data.title: Brooks Koepka quits LIV Golf for family reasons, Gooch to captain Smash GC in 2026
data.description:
Brooks Koepka, one of the LIV Golf originals, has quit the league ahead of the 2026 season, opening the door for a potential return to the PGA Tour.
The five-time major winner said he was leaving for family reasons in a statement released on Tuesday.
LIV Golf said the split was “amicably and mutually agreed”, adding that Talor Gooch would become the new captain of Smash GC, who Koepka had led since joining the breakaway tour in 2022.
“Brooks is prioritising the needs of his family and staying closer to home,” Scott O’Neil, the LIV Golf CEO, said. “We appreciate the significant impact he has had on the game and wish him continued success, both on and off the course.”
Koepka’s decision comes after his wife, Jena Sims, revealed in October she had suffered a miscarriage 16 weeks into her pregnancy.

Koepka’s tenure with LIV was laden with highs and lows. When he won the 2023 PGA Championship after spending most of his days playing ‍in LIV’s comparatively non-competitive environment that featured 54-hole, no-cut tournaments, it gave LIV a big boost of credibility.
On the ‍flip side, Koepka occasionally expressed his frustration with his situation – sounding like the 2017 and 2018 US Open champion and 2018, 2019, 2023 PGA titlist was marking time.
“I’ve ‌got a contract obligation out here to fulfil, and then we’ll see what happens,” Koepka said at one LIV ‍event.
His official third-person statement did not offer any hints as to his future.
“Brooks Koepka will be stepping away from LIV Golf. He is deeply grateful to Yasir Al-Rumayyan, Scott O’Neil, and the LIV Golf leadership team, his teammates, and the fans.
“Family has always guided ‌Brooks’s decisions, ⁠and he feels this is the right moment to spend more time at home. Brooks will continue to be a huge supporter of LIV Golf and wishes the league and its players continued success. Brooks remains passionate about the game of golf and will keep fans updated on what’s ahead.”
While Koepka can tee ‌it up for all four majors next year thanks to his 2023 PGA Championship victory, the PGA Tour requires LIV exiles to sit out one year from their last LIV tournament. His ‌last appearance was on August 24 ‌at the LIV Golf Michigan event.
In the 35-year-old’s absence, Gooch will take over as captain of Smash, and said he was “honoured and excited” by the opportunity.
O’Neil, meanwhile, called Gooch, who has won several times on the tour and was the 2023 individual champion, “a world-class talent ready for this leadership role”.
“This moment reinforces the healthier, more flexible global golf ecosystem that we’re striving to create,” O’Neil added. “One that supports players’ rights to compete on their terms while also showcasing our league’s competitive depth and the readiness of our players to step into leadership roles.”

data.title: China is now the good guy in climate change
data.description:
China draws flak in the West for seemingly everything it does. However, it’s the end of the year and we shouldn’t be so down on China, at least not all the time. So let me draw attention to a major development in the country that’s making the world a better place: clean energy.
That is a major “China shock” if ever there was one, but no one is putting it that way. Critics like to cite the stats that China burns as much coal as the rest of the world combined. That is true, but the country burns it to produce energy mostly to make the things the rest of the world buys cheaply.
In any case, China is so committed to weaning itself off coal that in the process it is creating world-beating technologies and industries in clean energy. This epochal energy transition is pushing many other countries to move away from coal, gas and fossil fuels, especially those in the developing world.
That is according to a recent report by the British clean energy think tank Ember. The non-profit research group concludes that China’s transition is “fundamentally reshaping the economics of energy across the world” by making clean energy and its infrastructure affordable to developing economies, not only within China.
The country is moving towards a peak in energy-related fossil fuel use by accelerating investments in renewables, grids and storage, along with the electrification of transport, buildings and industry.
Consider some stats cited by Ember. China is the world’s biggest investor in clean energy, spending US$625 billion in 2024, or almost a third of the global total of US$2.03 trillion. The volume of installed battery storage tripled in the three years to 2024. Grid investment rose to an all-time high in 2024 of 608 billion yuan (US$86.3 billion), up 25 per cent from 486 billion yuan in 2019.


Last year, investment and production in clean energy contributed 13.6 trillion yuan to the national economy, equivalent to about one-tenth of its GDP, or roughly the total GDP of Australia. The sector is growing three times faster than the Chinese economy overall.
How does China do it? This is state-led industrial policy at its best. Continuing the momentum built up across a decade, the latest 15th five-year plan – which runs until 2030 – seeks to replace energy from fossil fuels with renewables under the goals set by the Paris Agreement with the “dual carbon” tasks of reaching peak emissions by 2030 and carbon neutrality by 2060.
“These investments in the clean energy future are driving dramatic cost reductions across the world in key technologies such as wind turbines, solar panels, storage batteries and electric vehicles,” according to Ember.


By doing so, the country is also reducing costs and accelerating the uptake of clean-energy-powered technologies in other countries. “The benefits are increasingly being felt in emerging markets, many of which are overtaking OECD countries in wind and solar generation share and in electrification,” the Ember report said.
The prices of these technologies are down by 60 to 90 per cent from 2010. By 2024, the report said, more than 90 per cent of wind and solar projects commissioned worldwide produced energy more cheaply than the cheapest fossil fuel sources. Cheaper renewables for everyone are helping to pave the way for fossil fuel use globally “to peak and decline”, according to the Ember report.
Meanwhile, the United States is doing the opposite. The administration of US President Donald Trump has moved to end federal support for renewables in industry and research, and it is pressuring other countries to buy American fossil fuels in trade negotiations under threats of high tariffs. It is promoting the domestic oil and gas industry, including encouraging drilling in the Arctic. Trump has embraced the famous mantra of “drill, baby, drill”.

Chinese companies now supply about 80 per cent of solar panels and 60 per cent of wind turbines worldwide, according to Ember. Not only do US companies struggle to compete, they are actively discouraged by their own government from committing to renewables.
Maybe renewables don’t promote American democracy and free-market capitalism. However, they do help developing economies build wealth and better health for citizens, a different but essential kind of freedom.
It’s curious that the US government is run by a political party that denies – or at least plays down – climate change and is forcing the world to buy its oil and gas. Meanwhile, authoritarian China is helping the world adopt renewables.

data.title: Singapore Airlines passenger jailed for in-flight theft targeting business class
data.description:
A man who took a passenger’s luggage on a Singapore Airlines flight from Dubai to Singapore was jailed for 20 months on Tuesday.
Liu Ming, a 26-year-old Chinese national, pleaded guilty to one charge of theft.
The act was financed by a criminal syndicate and Liu had boarded the flight specifically to steal high-value items from business class passengers, the prosecution said.
His case follows other convictions for on-board theft this year, including one case of an offender who had accomplices on board the Scoot flight where he struck.
Liu was seated in the business class section of flight SQ495, which left Dubai for Singapore in the evening of August 7.
The victim, a 52-year-old Azerbaijani man, was seated five rows in front of Liu, while his wife was seated one row in front of Liu.
The victim and Liu did not know each other.
Liu acted in the early hours of August 8, when the dinner service had finished and the cabin lights had been dimmed.
At the time, the victim was asleep while the victim’s wife had just woken from a short nap. The cabin crew were in the cabin pantry with the curtains drawn.
Liu walked to the victim’s seat and opened the overhead compartment. He took the victim’s luggage and carried it back to his seat.
The victim’s wife saw Liu moving her husband’s bag and confronted him, asking whether the bag belonged to him.
As she did not understand his response, she alerted the cabin crew. While she did so, Liu returned the unopened bag to the compartment above the victim’s seat and returned to his own seat.
When the cabin crew questioned him, Liu lied that he had made a mistake and was confused about his luggage.
A search of the victim’s bag confirmed that no items were missing. The total value of items in the bag exceeded S$100,000 (US$78,000).
This comprised cash in various currencies amounting to about S$12,000; a Huawei laptop worth about S$2,100; 56 cigars worth more than S$5,400; a Chopard watch worth more than S$35,000; and an Audemars Piguet watch worth more than S$51,000.
Cabin crew notified the ground staff at Changi Airport and a police report was made. Liu was arrested upon arrival at the airport.
He was uncooperative during investigations, denying the theft and falsely maintaining that he had mistaken the victim’s bag as his own and wanted to get his toiletries.
This was even though Liu’s bag, which differed from the victim’s in appearance and material, was stored in the compartment above his own seat.
Deputy Public Prosecutor Cheah Wenjie sought 20 to 24 months’ jail for Liu. He argued for a stiff sentence because the offence engages the public interest.
“The proliferation of theft offences on board Singapore’s national carrier would tarnish its reputation, and that of Singapore’s tourist industry,” he said.
Cheah also pointed to the difficulty of detecting such on-board thefts, which has been highlighted in past similar cases.
This is because unsuspecting victims cannot keep a constant eye on their belongings on a flight and cabin crew also cannot monitor every passenger throughout the duration of a flight, he said.
The punishment for theft is up to three years in jail, a fine or both penalties.
This story was first published by CNA

data.title: Japan girl pop group member comes out as transmasculine non-binary after breast removal
data.description:
A member of the Japanese girl pop group Xtraordinary Girls (XG) has come out as transmasculine non-binary and revealed that she recently underwent breast removal surgery.
The seven-member idol group has made global waves with their bold blend of hip-hop and R&B, plus retro visuals inspired by Y2K and Harajuku styles.
Since their debut in March 2022, they have gained more than 4.5 million social media followers, with fans praising their “strong female energy” and lyrics.

The youngest member, Kokona Akiyama, known as Cocona, a 20-year-old rapper from Tokyo, once shocked the internet by shaving her head in the WOKE UP music video.
One fan said: “She does not chase a Barbie-doll look to attract fans. XG presents themselves as true artists.”
On December 6, while celebrating her birthday, Cocona came out as transmasculine non-binary on social media.

She revealed that she had undergone a mastectomy earlier this year and shared photographs of her scars.
The term transmasculine refers to individuals assigned female at birth but who identify as more masculine, while “non-binary” is an umbrella term for gender identities that fall outside the traditional categories of male or female.
In the post, Cocona said that being born and raised as a woman caused her deep discomfort.
“I live in a way that feels more masculine and natural to myself,” she said, adding that embracing her true self was once the hardest part of her journey.

“Now, I can finally say that there is nothing wrong with who I am inside,” Cocona wrote, expressing gratitude for the unwavering support from her XG members, parents and producers.
She hopes to create more with XG in the future.
“I hope my words can shine a little light on someone’s heart,” she added.
Cocona also said that she would prefer fans use “he/him” or “they/them” pronouns.
XG’s vice captain, Chisa Kondo, posted photographs with Cocona and captioned them: “It is Coco-chan day. No matter what, we will love you forever.”
In Japanese, chan, kun, or san can be used to refer to all kinds of people, regardless of gender.
Cocona’s actions have caused a buzz on social media.
One online observer said: “I am so proud of Cocona. This is his life, his body, and he has the right to do whatever he chooses. Cocona will become a role model for many, empowering them to embrace their true selves.”

While another wrote: “I support Cocona and am deeply moved by his bravery. Many trans artists in Japan cannot speak out. Cocona stood up and highlighted the importance of freedom and respect for different gender identities.”
But not everyone agrees.
One online observer said: “Cocona is only 20 years old, not fully mature mentally or physically. There are contradictions in her actions. By choosing to remove part of the body, Cocona may unintentionally reinforce the traditional views of ‘male’ or ‘female’ physical traits.”
Another person said: “How should Cocona fit in with the other six women in the group? Their group is known for promoting girl power. Does this mean Cocona should leave?”
A 2019 survey from nippon.com revealed that 9,625 people in Japan had officially changed their gender.
Although support for LGBTQ rights is growing among young people in the country, legal recognition still faces significant challenges.

data.title: China needs ‘original’ knowledge system on border history to counter West, scholar says
data.description:
China should establish an “original” knowledge system regarding the history of its border regions to mitigate security risks from “hostile forces in the West”, according to a senior scholar at a prominent state-run think tank.
Fan Enshi of the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences (CASS) also warned about the threat of “de-Sinicisation” in US-led historical perspectives, calling for a shift from “fragmented research” towards systematic domestic theories that could better project Chinese influence internationally.
“The world today is undergoing great changes unseen in a century, with hostile forces in the West in particular attempting to use borderland issues to contain China,” Fan, a deputy director of CASS’ Institute of Chinese Borderland Studies, cautioned in an article published last week by Qiushi, the Communist Party’s leading theoretical journal.
The efforts were “bringing more uncertain and unpredictable risks and challenges to the security, stability and development of our country’s border regions”, he said in the article published last week.

Fan went on to note that those circumstances necessitated a robust approach to developing the country’s indigenous academic system of Chinese borderland studies. However, “some fundamental concepts … remain unclear”, he said.
According to Fan, in contemporary terms, China’s land frontiers refer to areas within a certain distance of the country’s land border, generally covering nine provincial-level jurisdictions: the Inner Mongolia autonomous region, the Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region, the Tibet autonomous region, the Guangxi Zhuang autonomous region, as well as Yunnan, Gansu, Heilongjiang, Jilin and Liaoning provinces.
But he said the scope failed to align with two historical constructs: the peripheral regions under successive dynasties and the 1840 borderlands – the year marking the start of China’s modern era.
Given these inconsistencies, he called for “further in-depth study”.
China also faced an academic challenge in perfecting the country’s concept of maritime borderlands, he wrote.
That would involve incorporating the distinct rights for various maritime zones established by the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea – such as territorial seas, contiguous zones, exclusive economic zones and continental shelves, Fan said.
His article comes amid Beijing’s push towards “accelerating the construction of China’s indigenous borderland knowledge system”, following a call to action by President Xi Jinping during a Politburo study session in December last year.
China’s unique geography places it at the centre of 14 land borders – a distinction shared only by Russia – and eight maritime boundaries, where it neighbours several US allies, including Japan, South Korea and the Philippines.


In the South China Sea, territorial disputes with rival claimants continue to simmer and have sparked multiple clashes with Philippine vessels. Similarly, while the China-India Himalayan stand-off has cooled since last year, the underlying border conflict remains unresolved.
Following a high-profile central work conference in April, the Chinese leadership has prioritised relations with neighbouring countries while repeatedly denouncing what it views as interference by outside powers, particularly the US.
Meanwhile, Beijing has increasingly scrutinised ideas from abroad on China’s borderland history, previously largely confined to academia due to their potential negative impact on territorial disputes and ethnic minority governance in border regions.
In his article, Fan cautioned that certain historical works had failed to reflect the Chinese national perspective, attributing this to the influence of “traditional dynastic historiography and ‘de-Sinicisation’ theories from the US and other Western countries”.
These studies failed to establish a scientific discourse for the continuity between historical and contemporary China, he said, adding: “In the field of contemporary borderland studies, research results have become increasingly fragmented.”
He argued that Chinese borderland studies had relied too heavily on Western frameworks, sometimes overlooking China’s historical territorial integrity and the “unique” relationship between its central and surrounding regions in antiquity.
“[We should] strengthen our theoretical consciousness in constructing original and distinctive concepts in Chinese borderland studies,” Fan wrote, noting that the efforts should factor in “the long history of China’s territorial development” and the practical demands of contemporary governance.

data.title: Chinese ETFs investing in US stocks cap subscriptions as they run out of quotas
data.description:
China-listed exchange-traded funds (ETFs) targeting US stocks are capping new subscriptions, as strong demand from investors seeking diversification puts these products within striking distance of using up quotas granted by the regulators.
The nation’s biggest mutual fund firms that manage ETFs tracking the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100 index have imposed the restrictions, with these funds’ sizes approaching their respective quotas on overseas investments approved by the government. GF Fund Management is limiting subscriptions by an individual investor to 10 yuan (US$1.42) a day for its Nasdaq 100 ETF, with E Fund Management implementing the same curbs. China Asset Management stopped accepting new subscriptions since late November.
The spike in interest in US stocks underscores the diversification needs of Chinese households’ record 163 trillion yuan in savings, as the domestic equity market faces uncertainty and the property market continues its downtrend. Wall Street firms have predicted a third consecutive annual gain for US stocks next year, propelled by a continuing boom in artificial intelligence and further monetary easing.
“The resilience of US stocks is expected to carry into 2026 and the key driver will come from earnings growth,” said Zhang Yidong, an analyst at Industrial Securities. “In a base-case scenario, the AI narrative will deliver on earnings as the economy engineers a soft landing and the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates twice.”

He expected a 12 per cent gain for the S&P 500 next year, and the possibility of a further upside if the Fed reconciled with the Trump administration by delivering four rate reductions.
Beijing imposes restrictions on overseas investments by mainland-based investors, who can access US stocks only through fund products approved under the qualified domestic institutional investor (QDII) programme. Under the scheme, asset-management firms can exchange yuan-denominated proceeds from fund sales for foreign currency quotas to invest overseas. The combined approved quota stands at US$94.3 billion for QDII products offered by money managers and brokerages.
China had 22 ETFs targeting US stocks totalling 151.3 billion yuan, according to Bloomberg data. The biggest was the 29 billion yuan GF Nasdaq 100 ETF, followed by the Bosera S&P 500 ETF with US$22 billion in assets and the Guotai Nasdaq 100 ETF with US$16.6 billion, the data showed.
The Nasdaq 100 has risen 21 per cent this year, while the S&P 500 and the CSI 300 Index of China’s yuan-denominated onshore stocks have both logged gains of 17 per cent.
While Chinese stocks have outperformed many key global markets this year, its 2026 outlook is clouded by the poor latest monthly economic data and the possibility that stimulus measures may be underwhelming, with Beijing turning its attention to quality long-term growth rather than achieving short-term targets.
The S&P 500 may rise to 7,600 over a 12-month period, implying a gain of about 10 per cent from the current level, according to Goldman Sachs. Earnings for benchmark constituents would probably increase 12 per cent year on year in 2026, supported by 7 per cent revenue growth and a profit-margin expansion of 70 basis points, it said in a report this month.
The seven biggest S&P 500 stocks, including Nvidia, Apple and Broadcom, would contribute almost half of the earnings growth next year, according to the report.

data.title: Hong Kong firms hoping new southbound travel scheme will drive more business
data.description:
A new scheme that allows private cars from Guangdong province to enter Hong Kong’s urban areas has brought business opportunities for some, with users of a popular online platform selling courses on how to drive in the city or one-stop application services for mainland Chinese motorists.
Local businesses are also offering perks to drivers using the scheme, ranging from parking discounts to accommodation packages.
Under the “Southbound Travel for Guangdong Vehicles (Entry into Urban Area)” scheme, approved private cars from the neighbouring province are permitted to enter the city directly via the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge. The scheme, which started on Tuesday, allows 100 drivers to travel to the city every day.
Secretary for Transport and Logistics Mable Chan said that nearly 100 mainland cars entered Hong Kong on the first day. The first batch of cars entered the city via the mega bridge at 12.50am, transport authorities said.
One of the first drivers to arrive, Guo Jingyi, said the flexibility of a private car allowed her family to visit locations outside traditional tourist districts.
“Since we can drive ourselves now, we want to go to Sham Tseng to eat roast goose,” she said. “Usually, as tourists, we rarely go all the way out there, but having our own car makes it easy.”
The scheme has not only widened options for tourists but also brought new business opportunities.

In a post on social media platform RedNote, a user named “Hong Kong prices just expensive” offered “cross-border driving adaptation courses” specifically designed for Guangdong drivers heading south.
The courses were suitable for mainland car owners who had already applied for the cross-border scheme and wanted to drive themselves but were unfamiliar with the city’s road conditions, the post said.
It said Hong Kong-licensed instructors would provide bilingual guidance in both Mandarin and Cantonese and accompany drivers throughout their journey, teaching them how to handle the city’s unique conditions, including hillside roads, narrow streets and bus lanes.
Registrants can also choose their preferred routes, but the post did not provide the price of the courses.
Another post on RedNote offers agency services for applying for the scheme to save drivers the trouble of going through all the necessary procedures.
Under the scheme, applications must be submitted online to Guangdong authorities. Applicants must arrange prior vehicle inspections, obtain Hong Kong driving licences for the designated drivers and complete local licensing formalities.
Designated drivers will also be required to obtain third-party risk insurance to comply with Hong Kong laws.
The agency in the RedNote post charges 3,880 yuan (US$552), which covers services to help applicants apply for licences and insurance.
Some local businesses are also looking to tap into the new market, with shopping centres, such as Sun Hung Kai Properties’ flagship mall, apm, in Kwun Tong, offering perks including parking and shopping discounts.
Hotel COZi allows drivers who book rooms for two nights to pick different properties in Tsuen Wan, Kwun Tong and Tuen Mun on each evening to allow them to keep their itinerary flexible.
Last week, the Transport Department said that 700 mainland drivers out of 1,700 ballot winners had applied to enter Hong Kong’s urban areas under the scheme.
Approved Guangdong private cars can cross into Hong Kong at booked time slots via the mega bridge.

data.title: Yoon’s ex-aides in ‘disbelief’ over South Korean martial law plan
data.description:
Two former senior aides to South Korea’s ex-president Yoon Suk-yeol have testified in court that Yoon pressed ahead with a plan to impose martial law despite objections from cabinet members and top officials, with their statements offering a rare insight into the events that led to the short-lived decree.
Testimony by former National Security Office chief Shin Won-sik and former presidential chief of staff Chung Jin-suk on Monday shed new light on Yoon’s thinking behind his martial law declaration on the night of December 3 last year, which was swiftly overturned after lawmakers and protesters mobilised against it.
“The statements by Shin and Chung … show how Yoon immersed himself in delusional thoughts about what he was going to do, shutting himself off from others’ opinions as he recklessly forced through the martial law decree,” Jung Suk-koo, former executive editor of the progressive Hankyoreh daily, told This Week in Asia.
“[Shin and Chung] were apparently sidelined when Yoon began preparing martial law with assistance from three of his high school alumni, who were serving as defence minister, home minister, and commander of the military counter-intelligence command.”
Yoon and the three individuals have been taken into custody. The former president has been charged with masterminding the “insurrection”. The others, including former home minister Lee Sang-min, face charges for playing key roles.
While testifying in court at Lee’s trial, Shin said he had been “deeply disappointed” in Yoon, who lied to him about his plans to impose martial law.

Shin testified that on two occasions – at a meeting in March last year at a safe house near the former presidential Blue House in Seoul’s Samcheongdong district, and during a July trip to Hawaii for a Nato summit – the president hinted at imposing martial law.
For the March 2024 safe house meeting, Yoon brought together Shin, presidential security service head Kim Yong-hyun, spy agency head Cho Tae-yong, and Yeo In-hyung, commander of the military counter-intelligence command.
Earlier, Shin and Yeo said in a parliamentary hearing and during Yoon’s impeachment trial at the Constitutional Court that it was on this occasion that Yoon said for the first time he was considering taking an “extraordinary measure”.
During his meeting with Kim and others in Hawaii, Yoon attacked the opposition and denounced then-ruling People Power Party leader Han Dong-hoon, an estranged associate, as a “commie”, a derogatory label for a communist.
While in Hawaii, Yoon met Kim and deputy Joint Chiefs of Staff chairman Kang Ho-pil, instructing military leaders to prepare for martial law.
Upon returning to Seoul, Kang alerted then defence minister Shin Won-sik to the plan, prompting Shin to confront Kim.

A month later, Shin was abruptly removed from his post and replaced by Kim.
“[When I heard about the idea], I clearly conveyed my opposition [to the president],” Shin said.
“The president said it was something that had been talked about jokingly while drinking with Security Service chief Kim Yong-hyun, and I took Yoon at his word. But when martial law actually happened, I was deeply disappointed.”
Shin also testified about the cabinet meeting held just before the declaration of martial law, saying: “The mood was one of disbelief, and aside from Defence Minister Kim, I don’t think anyone supported it.”
Lee Jun-han, a political science professor at Incheon National University, said Shin had opposed the extraordinary measure “in the most obvious terms”, while other ministers “meekly expressed their opposition at best”.
“What Shin said appeared to be honest statements that were clear and concrete,” he said.
“His dismissal as defence minister is clearly linked to his opposition to the planned martial law imposition,” a move that consequently spared him legal accountability, the academic added.


Chung, who also appeared as a witness in Lee Sang-min’s trial, said he had likewise expressed opposition when Yoon gathered his cabinet ministers in his office moments before the martial law decree.
“I told the president it would be difficult to persuade the public. Citizens will pour out into the streets [in protest],” the former presidential chief of staff added.
Immediately after the decree, hundreds of protesters surged to the National Assembly to help lawmakers revoke the martial law decree, forcing Yoon to retreat. Street rallies against Yoon continued for months.
After fiercely resisting his arrest behind rifle-carrying security service agents at his fortified presidential office, Yoon was arrested in January but released in March on technical grounds.
Since being rearrested in July, he has remained defiant, maintaining his innocence and claiming his martial law decree was intended to “awaken” the public to threats from vaguely defined pro-North Korea, anti-state forces.
In his latest gesture of defiance, Yoon issued a Christmas message addressed to young people, in which he defended his martial law attempt.
“Jesus’ teachings are about the implementation of patriotism that fights tyranny against freedom,” Yoon said in the message, released from prison through his lawyers last week.
“One of the reasons that I declared martial law was the sense of desperation I felt that I must hand down a just country to the young people,” he said, expressing thanks to his supporters who have been staging demonstrations against his arrest.

The protesters are being led by Christian pastors who subscribe to right-wing extremist views.
Yoon’s message has sparked outrage from the ruling Democratic Party of Korea.
“The illegal martial law decree was not intended to protect freedom. Instead, it constitutes a serious crime that trampled upon freedom,” the party said in a statement.
“Without offering a single word of apology, Yoon is still denying reality, beautifying his crime as a sublime sacrifice. Especially his citing of Jesus’ teachings to justify his crime is an unpardonable blasphemy.”
Lim Ji-bong, an academic at Sogang University Law School, said that no matter how Yoon attempted to justify the decree, he had little chance of receiving a light sentence.
“He seems to be dreaming a pipe dream in his prison cell, which is unrealistic in light of the evidence, legal reasons and public opinion,” said Lim, adding that if Yoon were convicted as the mastermind of the insurrection, he would face either life imprisonment or the death penalty.
The ruling party is moving to introduce legislation to launch a new special prosecutor probe to further investigate scandals involving Yoon and his wife, Kim Keon-hee, which are likely to remain unresolved by existing special prosecutors as their six-month mandates are nearing expiration.
The bill aims to look into the involvement of government agencies and local governments in the “insurrection” and other outstanding issues.

data.title: 2026 Winter Olympics: ISU bosses leave speed skater Lam’s Milan dream hanging by a thread
data.description:
Joey Lam Ching-yan’s Winter Olympics dream hangs in the balance despite the Hong Kong short-track speed skater having qualified for the 2026 Milano-Cortina Games in February.
A last-minute change to the International Skating Union (ISU) quota allocation, which only hit women’s races, has left her just outside the qualifying list.
The 19-year-old ranked 35th in the Special Olympic Qualifying Classification (SOQC) for the women’s 1,500 metres, based on her top three results from four World Cup events this year.
Hong Kong Olympian Sidney Chu, who represented the city at the Beijing 2022 Winter Games, expressed his “extreme disappointment” with the ISU’s decision on social media, describing it as a blow to both fairness and transparency in the qualification process.
Under the ISU’s published qualification system, the top 36 skaters in women’s 1,500m secure Olympic starting places, which initially guaranteed Lam a spot at next February’s Games.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Sidney Chu | 朱 定 文 (@schu1)



However, the ISU confirmed earlier this month that the International Olympic Committee’s overall quota cap of 112 short-track athletes across both men’s, women’s and mixed events had been reached.
To stay within the limit, the ISU reduced the women’s 1,500m field from 36 to 33 skaters, cutting Lam from the final entry list released on December 10.
“I respect the complexity of managing Olympic quotas and the absolute limit of 112 athletes,” Chu posted on his Instagram page.
“However, it is deeply concerning that this adjustment resulted in a gender imbalance – not by one quota, but by three. When only female athletes are affected, explanations rooted in relay structure or generalised safety concerns feel arbitrary and difficult to reconcile with the decision.”
However, there is still a glimmer of hope for Lam.
Team Great Britain has announced that Niall Treacy will be its only short-track representative at the Games, effectively giving up two of its previously allocated places.
If those slots are redistributed, Lam could potentially regain her position when the ISU confirms the updated quotas in mid-January, just weeks before the Games’ opening ceremony on February 6.
Chu added that such decisions send a worrying message to athletes from emerging skating regions, who struggle with limited resources and fewer opportunities.
“These athletes already face limited resources and narrower pathways to qualification. When they earn their place through the established system, only to see that opportunity removed afterwards, it undermines confidence in the fairness and transparency of the process and risks discouraging future generations from pursuing the sport at the highest level.”
Chu, who has known Lam for over a decade, offered his support, describing her as “a role model to many kids in Hong Kong”, and praised her commitment.
“I know the sacrifice and dedication that she put into her craft. I am certain she would have made an excellent winter Olympian and would have done a much better job than I did four years ago,” he wrote.
“Joey Lam earned her ranking in the women’s 1500m through years of dedication, resilience and belief in that [qualification] system.
“Decisions like this do not just affect one athlete; they shape the future of the sport. I stand firmly in support of Joey Lam and all female athletes who deserve equitable treatment, clarity and a genuine opportunity to pursue their Olympic dream.”
Neither Lam nor the Hong Kong Skating Union were willing to comment.
The Milano-Cortina 2026 Games will feature nine short-track speedskating events: men’s and women’s 500m, 1000m and 1500m, the men’s 5000m relay, women’s 3000m relay and the mixed team relay.

data.title: China, Russia clash with US at UN meeting over Venezuela oil seizures and naval pressure
data.description:
China accused the United States at the United Nations on Tuesday of violating international law and destabilising the Caribbean region, as Washington defended its seizure of Venezuelan oil tankers and vowed to intensify pressure on President Nicolas Maduro’s government.
Speaking at an emergency meeting of the UN Security Council requested by Venezuela and backed by China and Russia, Beijing said US actions carried out under the banner of counter-narcotics enforcement amounted to unilateral coercion and a direct challenge to the principles of sovereignty and freedom of navigation.
“The US actions and the rhetoric have led to continued tensions in the region, raising serious concerns among regional countries and the international community,” said Sun Lei, deputy permanent representative of China to the UN.
“The US actions seriously infringe upon other countries’ sovereignty, security and the legitimate rising interests.”


The meeting came as the Trump administration escalated a maritime campaign in the Caribbean that has included the interception of Venezuelan oil tankers and a major US military deployment near Venezuela’s coast.
Earlier this month, US President Donald Trump announced what he described as a “total and complete blockade” of sanctioned Venezuelan oil shipments, framing the move as part of an effort to combat drug trafficking and transnational crime.
US forces have since seized two tankers and pursued a third. On Monday, Trump said the United States would keep both the vessels and the oil taken from them, suggesting the cargo could be added to US reserves.
China rejected Washington’s justification, arguing that the actions had no basis in international law and posed risks far beyond Venezuela.
Beijing has become Venezuela’s largest oil buyer in recent years, with crude often shipped through intermediaries, and Chinese officials warned that the seizures set a precedent that threatened global trade and maritime security.
The Chinese intervention also reflected broader concern over US policy in the Western Hemisphere. Sun pushed back against what Beijing sees as attempts to label normal economic engagement as a security threat, following language in the Trump administration’s National Security Strategy warning against “adversarial foreign influence” in the region.
“As an independent sovereign state, Venezuela has the right to independently develop mutually beneficial cooperation with other countries and defend its legitimate rising interests,” Sun said.
“China opposes all acts of unilateralism and bullying and supports all countries in defending their sovereignty and national dignity.”
Russia echoed that position. Ambassador Vassily Nebenzia accused the United States of “cowboy-like conduct” and said the blockade and tanker seizures violated core norms of international law.
“President Trump promised an end to conflict,” Nebenzia said. “Unfortunately, the situation is not improving, and the United States continues to sow chaos in the Western Hemisphere.”
China and Russia both urged Washington to de-escalate, citing the UN Charter’s principles of sovereign equality, non-interference and peaceful settlement of disputes. Sun called on the United States to halt actions that could further inflame tensions and to respect freedom of navigation under international law.
The dispute had already triggered a domestic response in Venezuela before the Security Council met.
On Tuesday, hours before the session in New York, Venezuela’s National Assembly debated and approved, at first reading, draft legislation that would impose prison sentences of up to 20 years on anyone who promotes or supports naval blockades or acts the government describes as piracy.

The bill also includes fines of up to one million euros and measures that provide legal and economic protections for shipping operators.
Venezuela’s ambassador to the United Nations, Samuel Moncada, used the council meeting to present his government’s case, accusing the United States of carrying out acts of aggression on the high seas.
He said US forces had unlawfully seized oil tankers engaged in legitimate international trade, detained civilian crews and threatened Venezuela’s territorial integrity.
Moncada described the tanker interceptions as “armed robberies carried out by military force in international waters” and said they amounted to an illegal naval blockade aimed at strangling Venezuela’s economy and forcing political change in Caracas.
He also alleged that more than 100 civilians had been killed in recent months during US operations against vessels Washington claimed were linked to drug trafficking, saying no evidence had been made public.
The United States rejected those accusations, with US Ambassador Mike Waltz saying Washington did not recognise Maduro as Venezuela’s legitimate leader and accusing his government of using oil revenues to finance criminal and terrorist organisations.
“The United States does not recognise Nicolas Maduro or his cronies as the legitimate government of Venezuela,” Waltz said.
“President Trump has been very clear that he is going to use the full power of the United States of America, the full might of the United States, to take on and eradicate these drug cartels which have operated with impunity in our hemisphere for far too long.”
Waltz defended the tanker seizures as lawful enforcement of sanctions in international waters and framed the US campaign as both a security and humanitarian effort.
“The people of Venezuela, frankly, deserve better,” he told the council.
